insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt eliminates the inference entirely — the purchase event is the signal, not a model of it.

With Chrome joining Safari and Firefox in deprecating cross-site tracking, the primary targeting primitive for most ad stacks is gone. Device fingerprinting is probabilistic and increasingly blocked, leaving a structural gap that first-party data alone cannot close.

Verified receipt attribution settles at roughly $0.10 per confirmed conversion versus $5–$150 CPA on conventional ad tech — a 50–200x cost difference that reflects eliminated infrastructure, not margin compression.

Amazon, Visa, Google, and Meta each see a fragment of the purchase journey. Receipt-layer data sits below all platform silos and captures confirmed transactions regardless of where the purchase originated or which ad stack was involved.

The dataset has moved beyond SaaS and subscription categories to include Home Depot and Starbucks receipts — signals that are harder to fabricate, higher in average transaction value, and more actionable for CPG and retail brand planning.

Consumer-submitted receipts enter the dataset whether a brand opts in or not, meaning brands that stay out are still being measured — just without any ability to shape, access, or respond to the data.
